title: Health | Beehive.govt.nz
content:
## New doctors to train in the regions that need them most
The regions that will host clinical training for the University of Waikato's new medical school from 2028 have been confirmed, alongside a new nationwide approach to clinical placements for medical students, Health Minister Simeon Brown and Associate Health Minister Matt Doocey say.

## Pharmac listens to feedback, and will fund ambulance medicines
Associate Health Minister with responsibility for Pharmac David Seymour welcomes Pharmac’s decision to fund medicines used by ambulance services from 1 July.

## Paramedics to be able to prescribe medicines
Paramedics will be given the authority to prescribe medicines, enabling more patients to be treated in the community and helping reduce pressure on emergency departments, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Ashburton community birthing unit upgrade underway
Construction is now underway on a major upgrade of the community birthing unit at Ashburton Hospital, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## First Rural Training Hub comes to the South Island
The South Island’s first Rural Training Hub, that will grow a pipeline of doctors, nurses and midwives for rural New Zealand, will soon be working on the West Coast.

## Life-changing cancer care closer to home in Northland
Construction of the new radiation oncology service at Whangārei Hospital is now complete, bringing cancer treatment closer to home for Northlanders, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Increased support for mothers and babies
Mothers and their babies will receive more support in the crucial days after birth, with increased maternity beds and more staff to support longer postnatal stays.

## Investing in cyber security to protect patient data
Cyber security across New Zealand’s health system will be significantly strengthened to better protect sensitive patient information and ensure services remain safe and reliable.

## Next step taken to lower bowel screening age
More than 200,000 additional New Zealanders will gain access to life-saving bowel cancer screening as the age of eligibility for free bowel screening is lowered further, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Infrastructure boost for hospitals
Major hospital upgrades at Whangārei, Tauranga, Hawke’s Bay, and Palmerston North, alongside investment in other facilities across New Zealand, will strengthen health infrastructure.

## Record health funding with patients at the centre
The Government is fixing the basics and building the future of New Zealand’s healthcare system, focused on delivering for patients and supported by record investment in health, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## NSNZ nurses vote to accept settlement
Health Minister Simeon Brown has welcomed the successful ratification of a new collective agreements for members of the Nurses Society of New Zealand (NSNZ).

## AI breast screening tool one step closer
The next steps are underway to introduce artificial intelligence (AI) into breast screening, supporting earlier detection and better outcomes for women, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Boosting ambulance services across New Zealand
More ambulance crews, upgraded technology, and stronger frontline support will strengthen ambulance services across New Zealand.

## Pharmac proposal to strengthen New Zealand’s vaccine response
Associate Health Minister David Seymour welcomes Pharmac’s proposal to strengthen New Zealand’s vaccine response, including by funding the flu vaccine for children aged 6 months up to 5 years old.

## Nelson Hospital parking expansion drives forward
Patients, visitors, and staff at Nelson Hospital will soon benefit from improved access and easier parking.

## Marking Pink Ribbon Day as extended breast screening reaches more women
Pink Ribbon Day is an important reminder of the value of early detection, with more than 16,440 eligible women aged 70 and 74 already taking up the opportunity for free breast screening since eligibility was extended last year.

## Rapid-build ward taking shape at Hawke’s Bay Hospital
The first modules for the new 28-bed rapid-build inpatient unit at Hawke’s Bay Fallen Soldiers’ Memorial Hospital have now been lifted into place, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Modern imaging technology for Southland Hospital
Patients in Southland will benefit from higher-quality diagnostic imaging following the installation of an upgraded fluoroscopy machine at Southland Hospital, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Nurses vote to accept new pay offer
Health Minister Simeon Brown has welcomed the successful ratification of a new collective agreements for members of the New Zealand Nurses Organisation (NZNO).

## Pharmac proposes improving access to type 2 diabetes medicines
Associate Health Minister David Seymour and Health Minister Simeon Brown welcome Pharmac’s proposal to change funding criteria for three type 2 diabetes medicines based on clinical need.

## New ED pharmacist role delivering faster care at Dunedin Hospital
A newly introduced pharmacist role in Dunedin Hospital’s Emergency Department is already helping patients get seen and treated faster, while giving doctors more time to focus on care.

## Bringing specialist paediatric palliative care closer
Children with serious illnesses and their families will be better supported with compassionate, specialist paediatric palliative care closer to home, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Cancer treatment closer to home for King Country patients
People in the King Country will now be able to access cancer treatment closer to home, with a new infusion service launching at Te Kūiti Hospital today, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.
